description: 'thephaku' is a local or whole-body inflammatory response pattern. Infection can prompt it, as can injury or immune activity without infection, so the inflammation does not identify its cause. Redness and warmth may describe one response; swelling, pain, or a finding from an examination may describe another.
articulatory_notes: Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th', pass air along it, and move into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Bring the lips close without involving the teeth for unvoiced bilabial 'ph', let air pass between them, and open into stressed central 'a'. Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into high-back rounded 'u'.
usage_notes: One localized instance can take a count. Bare 'thephaku' presents inflammation as a bodily process. The affected site and observations show its form; duration and a suspected relation place it in a course. 'peshiro' (infection) concerns an infectious agent, while 'hisaro' (clinical finding) concerns what a method observed.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Keeping inflammation separate from infection avoids turning every bodily response into language of invasion, impurity, or failure.

semantic_domains.physical: a local or systemic inflammatory response pattern
